primary_triggers: Operating System Version is Outdated
Operating System Version is Unsupported
Security vulnerabilities are present for the Operating system Version
Operating System is nearing End-of-Life (EOL)
Operating System no longer receiving security patches
Known exploit exists for current OS version
OS version incompatible with current hardware
OS version incompatible with required software or frameworks
secondary_conditions: Compliance requirements not met
Patch level behind current
Performance degradation observed on current OS
New OS version offers significant feature improvements
Vendor recommends upgrade for compatibility
Application stack requires newer OS version
OS version lacks support for containerization or ARM migration
exclusion_criteria: Operating System is marked as retired / decommisioned
Operating System updated within last 3 months
Is a SaaS Application
Operating System is part of a legacy system with no upgrade path
Upgrade would break critical dependencies
OS is running in a sandboxed or isolated environment
OS is used for archival or non-production workloads
Update is already scheduled or in progress
fulfilled_indicators: Operating system is on a current, supported version with no end-of-life or outdated status
Operating system is already fully patched and within its vendor support lifecycle

modernization_suggestion: Update the operating system to the latest supported version to address security vulnerabilities and compliance requirements.
reasoning: Outdated operating systems are more vulnerable to security threats and may no longer receive critical updates. Updating ensures ongoing support, patching, and compliance with industry standards.

scenario_name: Switch to standard Linux Operating System

primary_triggers: Operating System Type is Custom
Operating System is a commercial or proprietary non-Linux system (e.g. AIX, HP-UX, Solaris, IRIX)
Operating System lacks container support
Operating System is incompatible with ARM architecture
Operating System is not supported by cloud providers
Operating System has no long-term support (LTS)
secondary_conditions: Operating System has high maintenance overhead
Operating System Limited vendor support
Application is a custom developed Application
Security patching is manual or delayed
No automation tools available for current OS
OS version is not aligned with enterprise standards
exclusion_criteria: Application runs on Windows-based OS
Application is a SaaS Application
Vendor certifies application only on current non-standard OS
Application is tightly coupled to proprietary OS features
Application is certified only for current OS
Application is part of a vendor-managed appliance
fulfilled_indicators: Application already runs on a standard, widely supported Linux distribution (e.g. RHEL, Ubuntu, SLES, Debian, Amazon Linux)
Operating System is already a community or enterprise-supported standard Linux

modernization_suggestion: Standardize on widely supported operating systems that align with the customer's existing landscape standards to reduce the technology zoo, improve manageability, lower costs, and enhance security through simplified administration.
reasoning: Using a standardized Operating System simplifies patching, improves compatibility, reduces licensing costs, and increases operational agility.

primary_triggers: CPU Architecture is explicitly documented as x86
CPU Architecture is explicitly documented as x64
secondary_conditions: Operating System is Linux-based
Application Server is stateless (e.g. Tomcat, Spring Boot, Node.js)
Framework is ARM-compatible (.NET Core, Python 3.x, Go, Rust)
Deployment is containerized or Kubernetes-based
Target cloud environment provides ARM instance types (e.g. AWS Graviton, Azure Ampere Altra)
Application is stateless and loosely coupled
Application is well documented and actively maintained
Sustainability goals are defined
Is a custom developed Application
Cloud-native architecture is present
Security tools are ARM-compatible
exclusion_criteria: x86-specific dependencies
Application is a SaaS Application
Application is a 3rd party Application
Legacy software incompatibility
Application has 3rd party software dependencies that are incompatible with ARM architecture
Legacy Windows or proprietary Unix OS
Stateful or tightly coupled application servers
Proprietary or closed-source runtimes
Bare-metal or hardware-dependent deployments
Highly customized or undocumented applications
Hardcoded dependencies on x86 binaries
fulfilled_indicators: CPU Architecture is already documented as ARM or ARM64
Application is already running on ARM-based infrastructure (e.g. AWS Graviton, Azure Ampere Altra)

modernization_suggestion: Adopt ARM-based CPU infrastructure for better energy efficiency and cost savings.
reasoning: ARM-based CPUs offer improved performance-per-watt, cost efficiency and support sustainability goals due to lower energy consumption.

Application Server Status is End of Life

Application Server lacks container support
Application Server incompatible with ARM architecture
Application Server has limited cloud compatibility

High maintenance costs
Security vulnerabilities

Application requires modern runtime or framework
Application server lacks monitoring and logging integration
Application server is not compliant with security standards
Application server is not compatible with CI/CD pipelines
exclusion_criteria: Recent application server upgrade
Business-critical with no alternatives
Application is a SaaS Application
Application server is embedded in hardware appliance
Application server is part of certified vendor stack
Application server is used in isolated legacy environment
Application server is managed by external vendor contract
fulfilled_indicators: Application server is already containerized and optimized
Application server version is a currently supported, non-deprecated release

modernization_suggestion: Modernize application server infrastructure through one of the following approaches: migrate from commercial to open-source solutions to reduce licensing costs, transition to Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) managed services for simplified operations, or replace legacy application server technology with modern alternatives to improve performance and reduce overall costs.
reasoning: Legacy application servers often lack efficiency and support. Replacing them with modern alternatives improves reliability, reduces costs, and enhances scalability.

scenario_name: Application Migration to Cloud Infrastructure (Lift & Shift)

primary_triggers: Environment Type is On-Premise
Environment on Private Data Center
Application is virtualized
Application is containerized but not cloud-hosted
secondary_conditions: Servers are listed with a private Hosting zone
Application has predictable workloads
Application is stateless
Application is custom developed
Application is already monitored with cloud-compatible tools
exclusion_criteria: Application is an SAP application
Application is a SaaS Application
Specific hardware or regulatory reasons prevent Lift&Shift migration
Application requires physical appliances
Application has hardcoded IP dependencies
Application is tightly coupled with legacy systems
Application is part of a regulated environment with data residency constraints
fulfilled_indicators: Application is already hosted on a Public Cloud provider
Application is already part of a cloud-native architecture

modernization_suggestion: Deploy applications to public cloud infrastructure using a Lift(-tinker)-and-shift approach with modern deployment practices, maintaining existing architecture while gaining improved scalability, security, and compliance benefits.
reasoning: Migrating applications to cloud infrastructure with minimal architectural changes leverages cloud-native scalability, enhanced security features, and compliance frameworks while reducing infrastructure management overhead and operational costs.

primary_triggers: Used Programming language is legacy or outdated (e.g. Java 6 or older, .NET Framework 3.5 or older, PHP 5.x or older, Python 2.x)
Used programming language is no longer supported by vendor or community
Used Framework version is legacy or outdated (e.g. AngularJS 1.x, React <16, Vue <2, Spring <4, .NET Framework <4.5), STRUTS, JSF 1.x, ASP.NET WebForms
Used Framework version is lower than the currently supported major version (e.g. Angular < 10, Spring < 5).
Used Application Server is legacy or outdated (e.g. Weblogic 10.x, Websphere 7.x, JBoss EAP 5.x, Tomcat 6.x, IIS 6.x)
Used Application Server version is lower than the currently supported major version (e.g. Weblogic < 14, Websphere < 9, JBoss EAP < 7, Tomcat < 9)

Application has End-of-Life (EOL) indicators from vendor or community
Used conponents have known security issues or vulnerabilities that are not patched
secondary_conditions: Application is a custom developed application
Application Architecture is Monolithic and tightly coupled
Application uses CORBA or SOAP interfaces instead of REST or GraphQL
Application has poor maintainability
Application has no releases in the last 12 months
Application has high technical debt
Application has low test coverage or no automated tests
Application has performance issues or scalability limitations
Application has poor documentation or knowledge transfer
Application has high operational costs due to legacy infrastructure
Application has limited integration capabilities with modern systems
Application has low developer productivity due to outdated tools or processes
exclusion_criteria: Application is a simple applications with no scaling needs
Application is SAP Application
Application is SaaS Application
Application is 3rd party software — component versions (language runtime, framework, libraries) are vendor-managed and not upgradeable by the customer
Application is tightly integrated with legacy hardware
fulfilled_indicators: All application components (language, framework, application server, libraries) are on current, supported versions with no end-of-life or outdated status per technology assessment

modernization_suggestion: Rewrite or Refactor or Replace application for better security, agility and maintainability.
reasoning: Outdated components can be a security risk, leading to vulnerabilities and maintenance challenges.
